---
title: "Ben & Jerrys"
author: "Christopher Taratko"
date: "2023-11-02"
categories: [IceCream, code, data-analysis]
image: "ben-jerry-ice-cream.png"

execute: 
  warning: false
  message: false
  
toc: true
---

# Ben & Jerry's Ice Cream Dataframe Analysis

### Data Loading

```{r}
#| include: false
#| echo: false

library(knitr)
library(rmarkdown)
library(tidyverse)
library(skimr)
library(ggthemes)
library(hrbrthemes)

theme_set(theme_ipsum()+
          theme(strip.background =element_rect(fill="lightgray"),
                axis.title.x  = element_text(angle = 0,
                                            size = rel(1.5),
                                            margin = margin(10,0,0,0)),
                axis.title.y  = element_text(angle = 0,
                                            size = rel(1.5),
                                            margin = margin(0,10,0,0))
                )
          )
```

Let's analyze the `beer_data` data:

```{r}
ice_cream <- read_csv("https://bcdanl.github.io/data/ben-and-jerry-cleaned.csv")
rmarkdown::paged_table(ice_cream) 
```

## Summary of Data

This code provides the following statistics:

-   Mean (price)

-   Median (price)

-   Standard Deviation (price)

-   Mean (Coupon)

-   Mean (Household size)

```{r}
#| results: asis

mySummary <- ice_cream %>%
  summarise(
    mean_price = mean(priceper1),
    median_price = median(priceper1),
    sd_price = sd(priceper1),
    avg_coupon = mean(couponper1),
    avg_household_size = mean(household_size)
  )
data.frame(mySummary)
```

### Top 5 Ben & Jerry's **Flavors**

```{r}
ice_cream %>% 
  count(ice_cream$flavor_descr) %>% 
  arrange(-n) %>% 
  head(10)
```

## Regional DataGrouping

```{r}


regional_ice_cream <- ice_cream %>% 
  count(region, flavor_descr, name = "n") %>% 
  group_by(region) %>% 
  slice_max(n, n = 5)

regional_ice_cream
```

## Plotting

```{r}
ggplot(regional_ice_cream,
       aes(
         x = flavor_descr,
         y = n,
         color = region
       )) +
  geom_point(show.legend = F) + 
  facet_wrap(~ region, ncol = 4) + 
  ggthemes::theme_fivethirtyeight() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)) +
  labs(title = "Regional Best Sellers")
```

## Regression Model

```{r}
model <- lm(priceper1 ~ couponper1 + household_income +
              household_size + region,
            data = ice_cream)

unique(ice_cream$household_income)
library(stargazer)
stargazer(model, type = 'text')

```

### Explanation

Of this linear regression model, we can see that all except the south region is statistically significant. Another indicator we can use to help make valuable assumptions is the R\^2 value, where it is fairly low for this model, but that doesn't necessarily mean there's no correlation. It also seems that the household income has a very minimal effect, with the dependent variable being -0.00000. After researching possible reasons to this, it was concluded that the values within that column of data might be too large, and would have to be scaled back in order to get a visible number, so that it isn't cut off.

# Ben & Jerry's \[310 Addition\]

This section is designated to fulfilling requests of the Data Analytics 310 Homework assignment, as the rest that was above was for the 320 Homework assignment

<br>

### Regional Counts

```{r}
#| include: False
#| echo: false

library(tidyverse)
region_summary <- ice_cream %>%
  count(region, sort = TRUE)
region_summary
```

### Income Groupings

```{r}
#ice_cream$household_income
ice_cream <- ice_cream %>% 
  mutate(
    income_group = case_when( 
      default = NULL,
      household_income < 80000       ~ "Low",
      household_income < 150000      ~ "Mid",
      TRUE                           ~ "High" 
      ))

ice_cream %>%
  group_by(income_group) %>%
  summarise(
    avg_price = mean(priceper1, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_coupon = mean(couponper1, na.rm = TRUE),
    n = n()
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(avg_price))   # sorting

```

## Ice Cream ggplots:

### Price Distribution by Income Groups

```{r}
ggplot(ice_cream, aes(
  x = income_group, 
  y = priceper1,
  fill = income_group
  )) +
  geom_boxplot(size = .5) +
  labs(
    title = "Ice Cream Price Distribution by Income Group",
    x = "Income Group",
    y = "Price per Unit"
  )

```

Explanation: <br>

From the looks of it, low income groups (\$80,000 or less per household) spend more on average compared to their counterparts. This could be due to these lower income houses being either a single resident getting more expensive ice cream on average due to not having to take care of other individuals, or it could be some relation to how the ice cream is advertised in the lower income group's neighborhood

### Coupon Value & Price Paid

```{r}
ggplot(ice_cream, aes(x = couponper1, y = priceper1)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "gam", se = FALSE, aes(color = ""), show.legend = F) +
  labs(
    title = "Relationship Between Coupon Value and Price Paid",
    x = "Coupon Value",
    y = "Price Paid"
  )

```

Explanation: <br>

Initially, the coupon values are low, and scatter the price paid, but as they reach a point around \~\$3.25, the trend becomes very linear and only follows as it continues.

### Regional Price Distribution

```{r}
ggplot(ice_cream, aes(
  x = priceper1,
  fill = region
  )) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30) +
  facet_wrap(~ region) +
  labs(
    title = "Price Distribution by Region",
    x = "Price",
    y = "Count"
  )

```

Explanation: <br>

In terms of regional Price Distribution, you can see a very distinct occurrence the South Region being on the lower end, and spiking at that point. This can be attributed to it being a hotter climate most likely, and hotter climates bring people to demand more ice cream, so pricing ice cream lower will result in far more sales due to the season of ice cream having a much longer period of time, requiring people to be more conscious of their spending, and allowing them to spend more due to that different in price they would've paid elsewhere.

### Household Size vs Price Paid (Regionally)

```{r}
ggplot(ice_cream, aes(
  x = factor(household_size), 
  y = priceper1,
  fill = region
)) +
  geom_boxplot(size = .5, show.legend = F) +
  facet_wrap(~ region) +
  labs(
    title = "Household Size vs Price Paid by Region",
    x = "Household Size",
    y = "Price Paid"
  )
```

Explanation: <br>

As seen here, there's a more unanimous trend in Central and South, while the East & West almost split into two separate directions as the household size reaches 6 or greater. These don't have great explanations as to why, but it could be due to the type of population that lives in these regions, whether their households are built to be for family homes or for more luxurious purposes.
